does: This paper presents PEARL, a reinforcement learning framework for training LLM-based tutoring agents to provide effective Socratic guidance. The core contribution involves three technical components: a controllable student simulator that separates latent cognitive states from observable responses to capture diverse student abilities and misconceptions, a generative reward model that jointly scores pedagogical quality and correctness to guide policy optimization, and a stable multi-objective RL scheme that discretizes rewards per dimension and aggregates normalized advantages across dimensions. These components work together to address the challenge of training tutors that balance multiple pedagogical goals—such as progressive questioning, scaffolding, and conceptual depth—across multi-turn interactions while learning from synthetic student feedback.
connects: This work builds on a growing literature combining LLMs with educational applications and reinforcement learning for instruction. It extends prior research on student simulation by improving fidelity beyond binary correctness signals, responding to limitations in earlier work that used overly simplistic learner models. The pedagogical reward modeling connects to broader efforts in value alignment and multi-objective RL for complex tasks where ground truth is difficult to specify. The Socratic tutoring objective specifically builds on classical educational theory around guided discovery learning, while the multi-objective optimization approach relates to recent advances in stable RL for competing constraints and reward learning from human preferences in other domains.
enables: PEARL opens pathways for more sophisticated personalized education systems that can adapt to individual student needs and misconceptions. The controllable student simulator could be extended to incorporate real student interaction data, improving realism and applicability. The pedagogical reward modeling framework is generalizable to other educational contexts beyond tutoring—such as curriculum design, automated feedback generation, and assessment systems. The stable multi-objective RL scheme contributes methods applicable beyond education to any domain requiring balancing multiple competing objectives. Future work could incorporate real-time learning from human feedback, long-horizon student development tracking, and integration with concrete learning outcomes measurement to validate educational effectiveness in authentic classroom settings.
